title: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases
authors: Kim B. Yancey, Benjamin F. Chong, Thomas J. Lawley

items.subtopic: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases | PEMPHIGUS VULGARIS
items.title: Pathogenesis and Diagnostic Markers in Pemphigus Vulgaris
items.content: Pemphigus vulgaris (PV) is characterized by autoantibodies against desmogleins, leading to acantholysis. Diagnosis involves detecting IgG autoantibodies using indirect immunofluorescence and quantifying them with ELISA.
keyPoints: Acantholysis due to loss of cohesion between epidermal cells.
Nikolsky's sign is characteristic but not specific.
80–90% of PV patients have circulating autoantibodies detectable by indirect immunofluorescence on monkey esophagus.
IgG autoantibodies target desmogleins (Dsg3 in early, Dsg3 and Dsg1 in advanced disease).
Autoantibody titers correlate with disease activity.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases | PEMPHIGUS VULGARIS

items.subtopic: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases | PEMPHIGUS VULGARIS
items.title: Clinical Presentation and Prognostic Factors of Pemphigus Vulgaris
items.content: PV typically presents with fragile blisters on mucosal surfaces progressing to skin. It can be life-threatening, with mortality significantly reduced by glucocorticoids.
keyPoints: Fragile, flaccid blisters that rupture, often starting on mucosal surfaces.
Common sites include the mouth, scalp, face, neck, axilla, groin, and trunk.
Mortality rates have decreased from 60–90% to ~5% with glucocorticoid treatment.
Bad prognostic factors: advanced age, widespread involvement, high doses of glucocorticoids needed.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases | PEMPHIGUS VULGARIS

items.subtopic: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases | PEMPHIGUS VULGARIS
items.title: Treatment Strategies for Pemphigus Vulgaris
items.content: PV treatment involves systemic glucocorticoids, often combined with other immunosuppressive agents. Rituximab is effective in inducing remission.
keyPoints: Initial treatment: prednisone at doses ≤ 1 mg/kg per day.
If ineffective after 1-2 weeks, increase dose or add another agent like rituximab.
Rituximab with prednisone often achieves remission; maintenance therapy may be needed.
Other agents include azathioprine, mycophenolate mofetil, cyclophosphamide.
Severe cases may benefit from plasmapheresis or IV immunoglobulin.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases | PEMPHIGUS VULGARIS

items.title: Distinguishing Features of Pemphigus Foliaceus from Pemphigus Vulgaris
items.content: Pemphigus foliaceus (PF) is a superficial blistering disease characterized by acantholytic blisters located just beneath the stratum corneum, unlike pemphigus vulgaris (PV), which involves deeper layers. PF lesions typically spare mucous membranes and present as shallow erosions with erythema, scale, and crust formation.
keyPoints: PF is more superficial than PV, with blisters just beneath the stratum corneum.
Mucous membranes are almost always spared in PF.
Lesions resemble severe seborrheic dermatitis or extensive exfoliation.
Sun exposure may aggravate PF.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · PEMPHIGUS FOLIACEUS

items.title: Immunopathology and Associations of Pemphigus Foliaceus
items.content: PF is mediated by IgG autoantibodies against desmoglein 1 (Dsg1), a 160-kDa desmosomal cadherin. These autoantibodies can be quantified using ELISA. PF has associations with thymoma/myasthenia gravis and drug-induced cases, particularly from drugs containing a thiol group.
keyPoints: Autoantibodies in PF target Dsg1 (160-kDa desmosomal cadherin).
PF is associated with thymoma and/or myasthenia gravis (>30 reported cases).
Drug-induced PF often resembles the disease, especially from thiol-containing drugs like penicillamine.
Treatment typically involves systemic glucocorticoids or immunosuppressive agents.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · PEMPHIGUS FOLIACEUS

items.title: Diagnosis of Paraneoplastic Pemphigus (PNP)
items.content: Paraneoplastic pemphigus is an autoimmune mucocutaneous disease associated with neoplasms, characterized by painful stomatitis, papulosquamous and/or lichenoid eruptions progressing to blisters. Biopsy shows acantholysis, keratinocyte necrosis, and vacuolar-interface dermatitis; direct immunofluorescence reveals IgG and complement deposits on keratinocytes.
keyPoints: Associated with neoplasms like non-Hodgkin's lymphoma, chronic lymphocytic leukemia, thymoma, etc.
Biopsy shows acantholysis, keratinocyte necrosis, vacuolar-interface dermatitis
Direct immunofluorescence: IgG and complement deposits on keratinocytes
IgG autoantibodies to cytoplasmic proteins (e.g., desmoplakins I and II, BPAG1)
Pathogenicity confirmed by passive transfer studies in animal models
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · PARANEOPLASTIC PEMPHIGUS | BULLOUS PEMPHIGOID

items.title: Clinical Features of Bullous Pemphigoid (BP)
items.content: Bullous pemphigoid is a subepidermal blistering disease seen in the elderly, presenting with urticarial plaques and tense vesicles or blisters. It can be triggered by medications or neurologic diseases. Biopsies show subepidermal blisters; direct immunofluorescence reveals linear IgG/C3 deposits.
keyPoints: Commonly affects the elderly
Initial lesions: urticarial plaques, tense vesicles/blistering
Triggers include medications (e.g., furosemide), neurologic diseases
Biopsies show subepidermal blisters with eosinophil-rich infiltrate
Direct immunofluorescence: linear IgG/C3 deposits in the epidermal basement membrane
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · PARANEOPLASTIC PEMPHIGUS | BULLOUS PEMPHIGOID

items.title: Treatment Approaches for Bullous Pemphigoid (BP)
items.content: Bullous pemphigoid treatment ranges from topical glucocorticoids for localized disease to systemic glucocorticoids and adjuncts like doxycycline or rituximab for extensive involvement. BP may persist with exacerbations/remissions, and severe cases can compromise cutaneous integrity.
keyPoints: Localized disease: potent topical glucocorticoids
Extensive lesions: systemic glucocorticoids, possibly combined with doxycycline or rituximab
Disease course: months to years, with exacerbations/remissions
Severe cases may lead to widespread erosions and cutaneous integrity compromise
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · PARANEOPLASTIC PEMPHIGUS | BULLOUS PEMPHIGOID

items.title: Diagnosis and Differentiation of Pemphigoid Gestationis (PG)
items.content: Pemphigoid gestationis is a rare, nonviral subepidermal blistering disease occurring during pregnancy or the puerperium. It presents with polymorphic skin lesions that are intensely pruritic and can exacerbate post-delivery. Differentiation from other subepidermal bullous diseases is challenging via light microscopy but can be confirmed by direct immunofluorescence showing linear C3 deposits in the epidermal basement membrane, due to low-titer IgG autoantibodies against BPAG2.
keyPoints: PG occurs during any trimester or shortly after delivery.
Lesions are intensely pruritic and polymorphic (erythematous urticarial papules, vesiculopapules, bullae).
Exacerbations often occur within 24–48 hours post-delivery.
Direct immunofluorescence reveals linear C3 deposits in the epidermal basement membrane.
Autoantibodies target BPAG2, similar to Bullous Pemphigoid (BP).
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · PEMPHIGOID GESTATIONIS

items.title: Management and Treatment of Pemphigoid Gestationis
items.content: The management goals for pemphigoid gestationis include preventing new lesions, relieving pruritus, and caring for erosions. Treatment often involves moderate doses of daily glucocorticoids (20–40 mg prednisone) or potent topical glucocorticoids for mild cases. Systemic glucocorticoid use does not affect the incidence of uncomplicated live births compared to conservative management but may lead to reversible adrenal insufficiency in newborns.
keyPoints: Goals: prevent new lesions, relieve pruritus, care for erosions.
Treatment often requires 20–40 mg daily prednisone.
Mild cases can be managed with potent topical glucocorticoids.
No difference in live birth outcomes between systemic and conservative management.
Risk of reversible adrenal insufficiency in newborns if maternal treatment includes systemic glucocorticoids.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · PEMPHIGOID GESTATIONIS

items.title: Diagnosis and Confirmation of Dermatitis Herpetiformis
items.content: Dermatitis herpetiformis (DH) presents with intensely pruritic, papulovesicular lesions on extensor surfaces. Diagnosis is confirmed by direct immunofluorescence microscopy showing granular IgA deposits in the papillary dermis and along the epidermal basement membrane zone.
keyPoints: Lesions are symmetrically distributed over extensor surfaces such as elbows, knees, buttocks, back, scalp, and posterior neck.
Primary lesions include papules, papulovesicles, or urticarial plaques with prominent pruritus.
Direct immunofluorescence microscopy reveals granular deposits of IgA (with or without complement components) in the papillary dermis and along the epidermal basement membrane zone.
>90% of DH patients express HLA-B8/DRw3 and HLA-DQw2 haplotypes.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · DERMATITIS HERPETIFORMIS

items.title: Association with Gluten Sensitivity and Dietary Management
items.content: Almost all patients with dermatitis herpetiformis have associated gluten-sensitive enteropathy. A strict gluten-free diet can control skin disease, reduce dapsone requirements, and lead to the clearance of IgA deposits from the epidermal basement membrane zone.
keyPoints: >90% of DH patients have subclinical gluten-sensitive enteropathy.
Gluten-free diet can control skin disease and diminish IgA deposits over time.
Dietary gluten sensitivity is associated with IgA anti-endomysial autoantibodies targeting tissue transglutaminase.
Many months of strict dietary restriction may be necessary before beneficial results are achieved.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · DERMATITIS HERPETIFORMIS

items.title: Treatment and Management with Dapsone
items.content: Dapsone is the mainstay treatment for dermatitis herpetiformis, providing rapid relief within 24–48 hours. However, it requires careful pretreatment evaluation and monitoring due to potential side effects like hemolysis and methemoglobinemia.
keyPoints: Dapsone provides rapid symptom relief (24–48 h) in DH patients.
Patients require pretreatment evaluation for glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency.
All patients on dapsone at ≥100 mg/d will experience some hemolysis and methemoglobinemia.
Gluten restriction can lessen dapsone requirements, necessitating dietary counseling.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · DERMATITIS HERPETIFORMIS

items.subtopic: LINEAR IGA DISEASE | EPIDERMOLYSIS BULLOSA ACQUISITA
items.title: Distinguishing Linear IgA Disease from Other Subepidermal Blistering Diseases
items.content: Linear IgA disease presents with papulovesicles, bullae, and urticarial plaques primarily on central or flexural sites. It can clinically resemble dermatitis herpetiformis (DH), bullous pemphigoid (BP), or other subepidermal blistering diseases. Unlike DH, it does not associate with the HLA-B8/DRw3 haplotype or require a gluten-free diet.
keyPoints: Linear IgA disease can mimic DH, BP, and other subepidermal blistering diseases clinically.
Lesions typically appear on central or flexural sites; oral mucosal involvement may occur.
Severe pruritus is similar to that seen in DH.
Direct immunofluorescence shows a linear band of IgA (and often C3) at the epidermal basement membrane zone.
Circulating IgA anti-basement membrane autoantibodies target neoepitopes in BPAG2.
Treatment generally involves dapsone (50–200 mg/d), alone or with low-dose prednisone.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · LINEAR IGA DISEASE | EPIDERMOLYSIS BULLOSA ACQUISITA

items.subtopic: LINEAR IGA DISEASE | EPIDERMOLYSIS BULLOSA ACQUISITA
items.title: Understanding Epidermolysis Bullosa Acquisita and Its Management Challenges
items.content: Epidermolysis bullosa acquisita (EBA) is a rare, chronic subepidermal blistering disease. It can present as classic noninflammatory or inflammatory forms, with the former being mechanobullous due to trauma exposure. EBA shows increased HLA-DR2 frequency and may associate with Crohn's disease.
keyPoints: EBA is a rare, chronic subepidermal blistering disease.
Classic form involves blisters on noninflamed skin; inflammatory form resembles severe BP.
HLA-DR2 haplotype is more frequent in EBA patients.
Histology shows IgG (and often C3) linear deposits at the epidermal basement membrane zone, associated with anchoring fibrils.
Approximately 50% of EBA patients have circulating IgG autoantibodies against type VII collagen.
Treatment is generally unsatisfactory; options include systemic glucocorticoids, dapsone for inflammatory lesions, and other immunosuppressive agents like rituximab or cyclosporine.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · LINEAR IGA DISEASE | EPIDERMOLYSIS BULLOSA ACQUISITA

items.title: Recognizing and Managing Ocular Complications in MMP
items.content: Mucous membrane pemphigoid (MMP) can lead to severe ocular complications, necessitating aggressive treatment. Erosive conjunctivitis may cause scarring and potentially blindness if not managed properly.
keyPoints: Ocular involvement can result in shortened fornices, symblepharon, ankyloblepharon, entropion, corneal opacities, and blindness.
Aggressive systemic treatment is required for ocular, laryngeal, esophageal, and/or anogenital involvement.
Treatment options include dapsone, prednisone, or combination with immunosuppressive agents like rituximab, azathioprine, mycophenolate mofetil, cyclophosphamide, or IVIg.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · MUCOUS MEMBRANE PEMPHIGOID

items.title: Diagnosing MMP via Biopsy and Immunofluorescence
items.content: Biopsies of lesional tissue in MMP reveal subepithelial vesiculolobullae with a mononuclear leukocytic infiltrate, aiding diagnosis. Direct immunofluorescence is crucial as many patients lack circulating autoantibodies.
keyPoints: Biopsies show subepithelial vesiculolobullae and a mononuclear leukocytic infiltrate; early lesions may have neutrophils and eosinophils, while older ones show fibrosis.
Direct immunofluorescence reveals IgG, IgA, and/or C3 deposits in the epidermal basement membrane.
Testing perilesional skin is important due to lack of circulating anti-basement membrane autoantibodies in many patients.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · MUCOUS MEMBRANE PEMPHIGOID

items.title: Distinguishing Dermatomyositis from Other Connective Tissue Diseases
items.content: Recognize dermatomyositis by its distinctive cutaneous signs, which can help differentiate it from other connective tissue diseases like SLE and scleroderma. Key features include heliotrope erythema, Gottron's papules, and poikiloderma.
keyPoints: Heliotrope erythema: purple-red discoloration of the upper eyelids with scaling and periorbital edema.
Gottron's papules: violaceous, flat-topped papules over dorsal interphalangeal joints, pathognomonic for dermatomyositis.
Poikiloderma: areas of hypopigmentation, hyperpigmentation, mild atrophy, and telangiectasia, rare in SLE and scleroderma.
Approximately one-third of patients have Gottron's papules.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · DERMATOMYOSITIS

items.title: Management Strategies for Dermatomyositis
items.content: Treatment should be tailored to the severity of dermatomyositis, with options ranging from topical therapies for mild cases to systemic treatments for more severe disease.
keyPoints: Topical treatments include glucocorticoids and aggressive photoprotective measures.
Systemic treatment options: antimalarials (with caution due to potential drug eruptions), systemic glucocorticoids, methotrexate, mycophenolate mofetil, azathioprine, rituximab, or IVIg.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · DERMATOMYOSITIS

items.title: Acute Cutaneous LE: Systemic Correlation and Treatment
items.content: Acute cutaneous lupus erythematosus (LE) is characterized by a sudden onset of erythema in a butterfly distribution, often accompanied by systemic disease exacerbations. Skin biopsy reveals hydropic degeneration of basal keratinocytes and dermal mucin. Direct immunofluorescence shows deposits of immunoglobulins and complement.
keyPoints: Erythema in a 'butterfly' distribution with edema and fine scale
Systemic involvement often correlates with acute lesions
Skin biopsy: hydropic degeneration, dermal mucin, perivascular infiltrate of mononuclear cells
Direct immunofluorescence: deposits of immunoglobulin(s) and complement in the epidermal basement membrane zone
Treatment includes topical glucocorticoids, photoprotection, antimalarials, and systemic glucocorticoids
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · LUPUS ERYTHEMATOSUS

items.title: Subacute Cutaneous LE: Photosensitivity and Drug Triggers
items.content: Subacute cutaneous lupus erythematosus (SCLE) presents with photosensitive, nonscarring eruptions. It may resemble psoriasis or present as annular lesions. SCLE is often triggered by drugs like hydrochlorothiazide and calcium channel blockers. Skin biopsy shows epidermal atrophy and hydropic degeneration of basal keratinocytes.
keyPoints: Photosensitive, nonscarring eruption
Papulosquamous or annular polycyclic lesions
Drug triggers: hydrochlorothiazide, calcium channel blockers, antifungals, proton pump inhibitors
Skin biopsy: epidermal atrophy, hydropic degeneration of basal keratinocytes, mononuclear cell infiltrate
Direct immunofluorescence: deposits of immunoglobulin(s) in ~50% of cases
Treatment includes aminoquinoline antimalarial drugs and photoprotection
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · LUPUS ERYTHEMATOSUS

items.title: Discoid LE: Chronic Lesions and Progression to SLE
items.content: Discoid lupus erythematosus (DLE) features discrete, scaly lesions on the face, scalp, and ears. Long-standing lesions can lead to atrophy and scarring. Up to 20% of DLE patients meet criteria for systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE). Biopsy shows hyperkeratosis and follicular plugging.
keyPoints: Discrete erythematous papules or plaques with thick scale
Lesions on face, scalp, external ears; rare below neck without above-neck lesions
Long-standing lesions: central atrophy, scarring, hypopigmentation
Up to 20% of DLE patients meet ACR criteria for SLE
Biopsy: hyperkeratosis, follicular plugging, hydropic degeneration of basal keratinocytes, dermal mucin
Direct immunofluorescence: ~90% show deposits of immunoglobulin(s) and complement at the basement membrane zone
Treatment includes photoprotection, topical/intralesional glucocorticoids, and possibly antimalarials
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · LUPUS ERYTHEMATOSUS

items.title: Recognizing Scleroderma: Key Clinical Features and Diagnostic Approach
items.content: Scleroderma can present with skin changes that are either limited or diffuse, often beginning on the fingers, hands, toes, feet, and face. Important clinical features include recurrent nonpitting edema, sclerodactyly, Raynaud's phenomenon, and telangiectasias. The CREST syndrome is a specific subset characterized by calcinosis cutis, Raynaud's phenomenon, esophageal dysmotility, sclerodactyly, and telangiectasias.
keyPoints: Scleroderma often begins on the fingers, hands, toes, feet, and face with recurrent nonpitting edema.
Raynaud's phenomenon is documented in almost all patients and can precede scleroderma by many years.
CREST syndrome includes calcinosis cutis, Raynaud's phenomenon, esophageal dysmotility, sclerodactyly, and telangiectasias.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · SCLERODERMA AND MORPHEA

items.title: Differentiating Morphea from Scleroderma: Clinical Presentation and Management
items.content: Morphea is characterized by localized thickening and sclerosis of the skin, often on the trunk. It begins as erythematous or flesh-colored plaques that become sclerotic with central hypopigmentation. Unlike scleroderma, morphea does not typically involve systemic features but may be associated with other autoimmune disorders.
keyPoints: Morphea presents with localized thickening and sclerosis of the skin, often on the trunk.
Lesions begin as erythematous or flesh-colored plaques that become sclerotic with central hypopigmentation.
Skin biopsy of morphea is generally indistinguishable from that of scleroderma.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · SCLERODERMA AND MORPHEA

items.title: Identifying Eosinophilic Fasciitis: Clinical Features and Treatment
items.content: Eosinophilic fasciitis can be confused with scleroderma but is distinguished by sudden onset of swelling, induration, and erythema of the extremities. It often follows physical exertion or other triggers and involves proximal portions more than hands and feet. Key features include peripheral blood eosinophilia, increased ESR, and a 'groove sign' due to fascial involvement.
keyPoints: Eosinophilic fasciitis presents with sudden onset of swelling, induration, and erythema of the extremities.
Peripheral blood eosinophilia and increased ESR are common findings.
Treatment typically involves prednisone, with relapses managed by combination therapy.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · SCLERODERMA AND MORPHEA

items.subtopic: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases | PEMPHIGUS VULGARIS
items.question: Which autoantigens are targeted by IgG autoantibodies in patients with pemphigus vulgaris (PV)?
items.explanation: Patients with PV have IgG autoantibodies to desmogleins (Dsgs), specifically Dsg3 in early disease and both Dsg3 and Dsg1 in advanced mucocutaneous disease.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases | PEMPHIGUS VULGARIS

items.subtopic: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases | PEMPHIGUS VULGARIS
items.question: What is the significance of Nikolsky's sign in pemphigus vulgaris?
options: It indicates a positive result for direct immunofluorescence.
It suggests loss of cohesion between epidermal cells.
It confirms the presence of intraepidermal eosinophils.
It is specific to pemphigus vulgaris.
items.explanation: Nikolsky's sign, elicited by manual pressure leading to separation of the epidermis, suggests loss of cohesion between epidermal cells (acantholysis) and is characteristic but not specific to pemphigus vulgaris.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases | PEMPHIGUS VULGARIS

items.subtopic: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases | PEMPHIGUS VULGARIS
items.question: What histologic feature is typically seen in early lesions of pemphigus vulgaris?
options: Acantholytic blisters with round homogeneous cells
items.explanation: Biopsies of early lesions in PV show intraepidermal vesicle formation due to acantholysis, with blister cavities containing round homogeneous cells.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases | PEMPHIGUS VULGARIS

items.subtopic: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases | PEMPHIGUS VULGARIS
items.question: Which treatment is often combined with prednisone for achieving remission in pemphigus vulgaris?
items.explanation: Rituximab in combination with prednisone often achieves remission in patients with pemphigus vulgaris, though maintenance therapy may be required to prevent relapse.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases | PEMPHIGUS VULGARIS

items.question: Which protein is the target of autoantibodies in pemphigus foliaceus?
items.explanation: Autoantibodies in pemphigus foliaceus are directed against Dsg1, a desmosomal cadherin.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · PEMPHIGUS FOLIACEUS

items.question: What is the typical location of acantholytic blisters in pemphigus foliaceus?
items.explanation: Acantholytic blisters in pemphigus foliaceus are located high within the epidermis, usually just beneath the stratum corneum.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · PEMPHIGUS FOLIACEUS

items.question: Which of the following is a common aggravating factor for pemphigus foliaceus?
items.explanation: Sun exposure (ultraviolet irradiation) may be an aggravating factor for pemphigus foliaceus.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · PEMPHIGUS FOLIACEUS

items.question: Which of the following neoplasms is particularly common among children with paraneoplastic pemphigus?
items.explanation: Castleman's disease is particularly common among children with paraneoplastic pemphigus.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · PARANEOPLASTIC PEMPHIGUS | BULLOUS PEMPHIGOID

items.question: What type of immunofluorescence microscopy finding is seen in the skin of patients with bullous pemphigoid?
options: Granular deposits of IgA and C3 in the dermis
Linear deposits of IgG and/or C3 in the epidermal basement membrane
Focal deposits of IgM on keratinocytes
items.explanation: Bullous pemphigoid shows linear deposits of IgG and/or C3 in the epidermal basement membrane.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · PARANEOPLASTIC PEMPHIGUS | BULLOUS PEMPHIGOID

items.question: Which protein is NOT a target of autoantibodies in paraneoplastic pemphigus?
items.explanation: Paraneoplastic pemphigus involves autoantibodies to desmoplakin I, BPAG1, and Dsg3, but not collagen IV.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · PARANEOPLASTIC PEMPHIGUS | BULLOUS PEMPHIGOID

items.question: Which of the following is a known trigger for bullous pemphigoid?
items.explanation: Bullous pemphigoid can be triggered by medications such as furosemide.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · PARANEOPLASTIC PEMPHIGUS | BULLOUS PEMPHIGOID

items.question: At what time point do severe exacerbations of pemphigoid gestationis (PG) frequently occur?
options: During the first trimester of pregnancy
items.explanation: Severe exacerbations of PG frequently follow delivery, typically within 24–48 hours.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · PEMPHIGOID GESTATIONIS

items.question: What is the immunopathologic hallmark of pemphigoid gestationis (PG) revealed by direct immunofluorescence microscopy?
options: Linear deposits of IgG in the epidermal basement membrane
Granular deposits of C3 in the dermis
Linear deposits of C3 in the epidermal basement membrane
items.explanation: Direct immunofluorescence microscopy reveals linear deposits of C3 in the epidermal basement membrane.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · PEMPHIGOID GESTATIONIS

items.question: Which protein is targeted by autoantibodies in both pemphigoid gestationis (PG) and bullous pemphigoid (BP)?
items.explanation: Autoantibodies in both PG and BP target BPAG2, a he�-mesomosome-associated protein.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · PEMPHIGOID GESTATIONIS

items.question: Which HLA haplotypes are most commonly expressed in patients with dermatitis herpetiformis?
items.explanation: The passage states that >90% of DH patients express the HLA-B8/DRw3 and HLA-DQw2 haplotypes.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · DERMATITIS HERPETIFORMIS

items.question: What is the primary treatment for dermatitis herpetiformis?
items.explanation: The passage identifies dapsone as the mainstay of treatment for DH.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · DERMATITIS HERPETIFORMIS

items.question: What is a characteristic histologic finding in early lesions of dermatitis herpetiformis?
items.explanation: The passage mentions that biopsy of early lesional skin reveals neutrophil-rich infiltrates within dermal papillae.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · DERMATITIS HERPETIFORMIS

items.question: What dietary intervention can lead to the clearance of IgA deposits in patients with dermatitis herpetiformis?
items.explanation: The passage states that a strict gluten-free diet can lead to the clearance of IgA deposits in DH patients.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · DERMATITIS HERPETIFORMIS

items.subtopic: LINEAR IGA DISEASE | EPIDERMOLYSIS BULLOSA ACQUISITA
items.question: Which immunoglobulin is typically found in a linear band at the epidermal basement membrane zone in patients with Linear IgA disease?
items.explanation: Direct immunofluorescence microscopy of linear IgA disease reveals a linear band of IgA in the epidermal basement membrane zone.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · LINEAR IGA DISEASE | EPIDERMOLYSIS BULLOSA ACQUISITA

items.subtopic: LINEAR IGA DISEASE | EPIDERMOLYSIS BULLOSA ACQUISITA
items.question: Which treatment is commonly used for Linear IgA disease?
items.explanation: Patients with linear IgA disease generally respond to treatment with dapsone.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · LINEAR IGA DISEASE | EPIDERMOLYSIS BULLOSA ACQUISITA

items.subtopic: LINEAR IGA DISEASE | EPIDERMOLYSIS BULLOSA ACQUISITA
items.question: What is a distinguishing feature of the histologic alterations in early lesions of Linear IgA disease?
options: Linear band of IgG and C3
Continuous deposits of IgG in a linear pattern
items.explanation: Histologic alterations in early lesions of Linear IgA disease may be virtually indistinguishable from those in DH.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · LINEAR IGA DISEASE | EPIDERMOLYSIS BULLOSA ACQUISITA

items.question: Which type of immunoglobulin is typically found in direct immunofluorescence microscopy of perilesional tissue in mucous membrane pemphigoid?
items.explanation: Direct immunofluorescence typically reveals deposits of IgG, IgA, and/or C3 in the epidermal basement membrane.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · MUCOUS MEMBRANE PEMPHIGOID

items.question: Which site is most commonly affected by mucous membrane pemphigoid?
items.explanation: Common sites include the oral mucosa, especially the gingiva.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · MUCOUS MEMBRANE PEMPHIGOID

items.question: What is a potential serious complication of erosive conjunctivitis in patients with mucous membrane pemphigoid?
items.explanation: Erosive conjunctivitis may result in blindness in severe cases.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · MUCOUS MEMBRANE PEMPHIGOID

items.question: Which of the following is a pathognomonic cutaneous manifestation of dermatomyositis?
options: Erythema on the cheeks and nose in a 'butterfly' distribution
Violaceous, flat-topped papules over the dorsal interphalangeal joints
Erythematous or violaceous thin, scaly plaques on the upper trunk
items.explanation: Gottron's papules are pathognomonic for dermatomyositis.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · DERMATOMYOSITIS

items.question: Which clinical feature can help distinguish dermatomyositis from systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE)?
options: Erythema on the cheeks and nose in a 'butterfly' distribution
items.explanation: Poikiloderma, including periungual changes, is more common in dermatomyositis than SLE.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · DERMATOMYOSITIS

items.question: Which of the following treatments is NOT typically used for systemic disease management in dermatomyositis?
items.explanation: Beta-blockers are not used for systemic disease management in dermatomyositis.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · DERMATOMYOSITIS

items.question: Which cutaneous manifestation of LE typically presents with erythema in a 'butterfly' distribution?
items.explanation: Acute cutaneous LE is characterized by erythema of the nose and malar eminences in a 'butterfly' distribution.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · LUPUS ERYTHEMATOSUS

items.question: Which type of lupus erythematosus often presents with papulosquamous eruptions that resemble psoriasis?
items.explanation: Subacute cutaneous lupus erythematosus (SCLE) may present as a papulosquamous eruption that resembles psoriasis.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · LUPUS ERYTHEMATOSUS

items.question: Which type of LE is associated with lesions characterized by 'carpet tacking'?
items.explanation: 'Carpet tacking' is a finding relatively specific for discoid lupus erythematosus (DLE).
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · LUPUS ERYTHEMATOSUS

items.question: Which type of LE typically requires treatment with aminoquinoline antimalarial drugs?
items.explanation: Most patients with subacute cutaneous LE require treatment with aminoquinoline antimalarial drugs.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · LUPUS ERYTHEMATOSUS

items.question: Which of the following is a common early sign in patients with scleroderma that may precede skin changes by many years?
items.explanation: Raynaud's phenomenon is documented in almost all patients with scleroderma and can precede skin changes by many years.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · SCLERODERMA AND MORPHEA

items.question: Which autoantibody is most commonly associated with CREST syndrome?
items.explanation: Anti-centromere autoantibodies have been reported in a very high percentage of patients with CREST syndrome.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · SCLERODERMA AND MORPHEA

items.question: What is the characteristic appearance of skin affected by eosinophilic fasciitis?
items.explanation: The skin in eosinophilic fasciitis usually displays a woody, dimpled, or 'pseudocellulite' appearance.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · SCLERODERMA AND MORPHEA

items.question: Which of the following is NOT typically involved in CREST syndrome?
items.explanation: Hyperpigmentation is not a component of the CREST syndrome.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · SCLERODERMA AND MORPHEA

items.subtopic: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases | PEMPHIGUS VULGARIS
items.statement: Pemphigus vulgaris typically begins on the skin and progresses to mucosal surfaces.
items.explanation: PV typically begins on mucosal surfaces and often progresses to involve the skin.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases | PEMPHIGUS VULGARIS

items.subtopic: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases | PEMPHIGUS VULGARIS
items.statement: The mortality rate of pemphigus vulgaris has decreased significantly with the availability of glucocorticoids.
items.explanation: Prior to glucocorticoids, mortality rates were 60-90%, but currently it is around 5%.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases | PEMPHIGUS VULGARIS

items.statement: Mucous membranes are commonly affected in pemphigus foliaceus.
items.explanation: In pemphigus foliaceus, mucous membranes are almost always spared.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · PEMPHIGUS FOLIACEUS

items.statement: Drug-induced pemphigus often resembles pemphigus vulgaris.
items.explanation: Drug-induced pemphigus usually resembles pemphigus foliaceus rather than pemphigus vulgaris.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · PEMPHIGUS FOLIACEUS

items.statement: Paraneoplastic pemphigus is generally resistant to conventional therapies used for pemphigus vulgaris.
items.explanation: PNP is generally resistant to conventional therapies used for PV.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · PARANEOPLASTIC PEMPHIGUS | BULLOUS PEMPHIGOID

items.statement: Bullous pemphigoid is characterized by subepidermal blisters with a sparse perivascular leukocytic infiltrate.
items.explanation: Early lesions of BP show subepidermal blisters and a sparse perivascular leukocytic infiltrate.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · PARANEOPLASTIC PEMPHIGUS | BULLOUS PEMPHIGOID

items.statement: Pemphigoid gestationis (PG) is caused by a viral infection.
items.explanation: PG is a nonviral, subepidermal blistering disease.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · PEMPHIGOID GESTATIONIS

items.statement: Infants of mothers with PG may have transient skin lesions.
items.explanation: Occasionally, infants born to affected mothers develop transient skin lesions.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · PEMPHIGOID GESTATIONIS

items.statement: Dermatitis herpetiformis lesions are primarily found on flexor surfaces.
items.explanation: The passage specifies that DH lesions are symmetrically distributed over extensor surfaces.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · DERMATITIS HERPETIFORMIS

items.statement: A gluten-free diet can control dermatitis herpetiformis and reduce the need for dapsone.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that a gluten-free diet can control DH and lessen dapsone requirements.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · DERMATITIS HERPETIFORMIS

items.subtopic: LINEAR IGA DISEASE | EPIDERMOLYSIS BULLOSA ACQUISITA
items.statement: Patients with Epidermolysis Bullosa Acquisita have an increased frequency of the HLA-B8/DRw3 haplotype.
items.explanation: The HLA-DR2 haplotype, not HLA-B8/DRw3, is found with increased frequency in EBA patients.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · LINEAR IGA DISEASE | EPIDERMOLYSIS BULLOSA ACQUISITA

items.subtopic: LINEAR IGA DISEASE | EPIDERMOLYSIS BULLOSA ACQUISITA
items.statement: Linear IgA disease can be treated effectively with a gluten-free diet.
items.explanation: Patients with linear IgA disease are not candidates for treatment with a gluten-free diet.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · LINEAR IGA DISEASE | EPIDERMOLYSIS BULLOSA ACQUISITA

items.statement: Mucous membrane pemphigoid is typically a self-limiting disorder.
items.explanation: MMP is typically a chronic and progressive disorder.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · MUCOUS MEMBRANE PEMPHIGOID

items.statement: Patients with mucous membrane pemphigoid may have autoantibodies to laminin-332.
items.explanation: Studies suggest that MMP patients with autoantibodies to laminin-332 have an increased relative risk for cancer.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · MUCOUS MEMBRANE PEMPHIGOID

items.statement: Dermatomyositis can present with interstitial lung disease or cancer.
items.explanation: Dermatomyositis is associated with interstitial lung disease and malignancies.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · DERMATOMYOSITIS

items.statement: Direct immunofluorescence microscopy of lesional skin in dermatomyositis is always positive.
items.explanation: Direct immunofluorescence microscopy is usually negative, though some cases may show deposits.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · DERMATOMYOSITIS

items.statement: Direct immunofluorescence microscopy of lesional skin in acute cutaneous LE frequently reveals deposits of immunoglobulin(s) and complement in the epidermal basement membrane zone.
items.explanation: This statement is accurate for acute cutaneous LE.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · LUPUS ERYTHEMATOSUS

items.statement: Renal and central nervous system involvement are common in patients with subacute cutaneous lupus erythematosus.
items.explanation: In most patients with SCLE, renal and central nervous system involvement is mild or absent.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · LUPUS ERYTHEMATOSUS

items.statement: Morphea often involves widespread cutaneous lesions without systemic involvement.
items.explanation: Generalized morphea can present with widespread cutaneous lesions without systemic involvement.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · SCLERODERMA AND MORPHEA

items.statement: Direct immunofluorescence microscopy of lesional skin in scleroderma is usually positive.
items.explanation: Direct immunofluorescence microscopy of lesional skin in scleroderma is usually negative.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · SCLERODERMA AND MORPHEA

items.subtopic: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases | PEMPHIGUS VULGARIS
items.reason: The disease can lead to severe complications such as infection and treatment-related issues.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why pemphigus vulgaris can be life-threatening.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases | PEMPHIGUS VULGARIS

items.subtopic: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases | PEMPHIGUS VULGARIS
items.assertion: Patients with early pemphigus vulgaris have IgG autoantibodies to Dsg1.
items.reason: Dsg1 is the primary target in all stages of pemphigus vulgaris.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; patients with early PV (mucosal disease) have IgG autoantibodies to Dsg3, not Dsg1.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases | PEMPHIGUS VULGARIS

items.assertion: Paraneoplastic pemphigus can be associated with life-threatening bronchiolitis obliterans.
items.reason: PNP involves severe skin lesions and systemic complications including bronchiolitis obliterans.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · PARANEOPLASTIC PEMPHIGUS | BULLOUS PEMPHIGOID

items.assertion: Bullous pemphigoid is commonly associated with neurologic diseases.
items.reason: BP has been linked to conditions such as stroke, dementia, and Parkinson's disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the association.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · PARANEOPLASTIC PEMPHIGUS | BULLOUS PEMPHIGOID

items.assertion: Dermatitis herpetiformis is associated with gluten-sensitive enteropathy.
items.reason: Almost all DH patients have subclinical gluten-sensitive enteropathy.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the association.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · DERMATITIS HERPETIFORMIS

items.assertion: Dermatitis herpetiformis is primarily treated with topical corticosteroids.
items.reason: Topical corticosteroids reduce inflammation in dermatologic conditions.
items.explanation: The assertion is false as dapsone, not topical corticosteroids, is the primary treatment for DH.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · DERMATITIS HERPETIFORMIS

items.assertion: Patients with discoid LE often have lesions below the neck without lesions above the neck.
items.reason: It is unusual to see DLE lesions below the neck without lesions above the neck.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; it is unusual for DLE lesions to be found below the neck without being present above.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · LUPUS ERYTHEMATOSUS

items.assertion: Up to 20% of patients with discoid LE eventually meet the American College of Rheumatology criteria for SLE.
items.reason: Discoid lesions are frequently seen in patients with SLE.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why up to 20% of DLE patients meet SLE criteria.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · LUPUS ERYTHEMATOSUS

items.assertion: Patients with eosinophilic fasciitis are at increased risk for developing bone marrow failure.
items.reason: Eosinophilic fasciitis is associated with peripheral blood eosinophilia and other hematologic abnormalities.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · SCLERODERMA AND MORPHEA

items.assertion: Morphea is characterized by diffuse thickening of skin over the trunk.
items.reason: Morphea typically presents as localized plaques that become sclerotic.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because morphea involves localized rather than diffuse thickening.
items.reference: Immunologically Mediated Skin Diseases · SCLERODERMA AND MORPHEA
